content:
9 min read
Just now

--

Learn how to install Git and use basic Git commands like git init, git add, git commit, git push, git pull, git clone, and git merge through this simple step-by-step tutorial.
Introduction
If you’re new to programming or software development, you’ve probably heard about Git. At first, Git may seem confusing because it has many commands, but once you understand the basic workflow, it becomes one of the most useful tools for managing projects.
In this tutorial, I’ll show you how to install Git and use the most important Git commands step by step. Even if you’ve never used Git before, you can follow this guide and practice everything on your own.
By the end of this tutorial, you’ll know how to:

• Install Git

• Configure Git

• Create your first repository

• Stage and commit files

• Push your project to GitHub

• Clone a repository

• Pull the latest changes

• Create and merge branches
Let’s get started!
What is Git?
Git is a Version Control System (VCS). It helps developers keep track of changes made to a project.
Instead of creating multiple copies of the same project every time you make changes, Git stores every version in an organized way. This makes it easy to go back to previous versions if needed.
Git is useful because it helps you:

• Track changes in your project.

• Work on new features safely.

• Restore previous versions.

• Collaborate with other developers.
Git vs GitHub
GitGitHubGit and GitHub are related, but they are not the same. Git is a version control system that helps you track and manage changes to your project on your computer. GitHub, on the other hand, is an online platform that stores Git repositories in the cloud, making it easy to back up your work and collaborate with others. In simple terms, Git manages your project locally, while GitHub helps you share and access it online.
Prerequisites
Before you begin, make sure you have:

• A GitHub account

• Visual Studio Code (or any code editor)

• A terminal (Command Prompt, PowerShell, Terminal, or Git Bash)
Now let’s install Git.
Step 1: Download and Install Git
Open your browser and visit the official Git website.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Download the version of Git that matches your device. Once the download is complete, open the installer and follow the on-screen instructions.
If you’re unsure which options to choose during installation, simply continue with the default settings. They work well for most users.
After the installation is complete, click Finish.
Step 2: Verify the Installation
Now let’s check if Git was installed successfully.
Open your terminal and type:
git — versionPress Enter.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

If Git is installed correctly, you’ll see an output similar to this:
git version 2.55.0.windows.1Your version number may be different, and that’s completely fine.
Step 3: Configure Git
Before using Git, you need to tell Git who you are. This information is saved with every commit you make.
Run the following command to set your username:
git config — global user.name “Your Name”Next, set your email address:
git config — global user.email “your@email.com”Replace the example values with your own username and email address.
To check whether the configuration was successful, run:
git config — listYou should now see your username and email in the list.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 4: Create Your First Git Repository
Now it’s time to create your first Git project.
Create a new folder anywhere on your computer. For this tutorial, I’ll use the folder name GitPractice2.
Open the folder in Visual Studio Code.
Next, open the terminal by selecting:
Terminal → New Terminal
To turn this folder into a Git repository, type:
git initPress Enter.
This command tells Git to start tracking your project. After running it, Git creates a hidden .git folder that stores all the information related to your repository.
Now check the repository status by running:
git statusSince this is a new repository, Git will show that there are no commits yet.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 5: Create Your First File and Check the Status
Now let’s create our first file.
Inside your project folder, create a new text file named hello.txt.
Open the file and type a few lines, for example:
Hello, Git!
This is my first Git project.
Save the file.
Now go back to the terminal and check the repository status by running:
git statusYou’ll notice that Git displays hello.txt under Untracked files.
This means Git has found the file, but it is not tracking it yet.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 6: Stage the File
Before saving changes permanently, Git asks us to move the file to the staging area.
To do that, run:
git add .The dot (.) tells Git to stage all the files in the current folder.
Now check the status again:
git statusThis time, Git will show that the file is ready to be committed.
What is the Staging Area?
Think of the staging area as a waiting area. It lets you choose which changes should be included in your next commit.
Step 7: Unstage a File
Sometimes you may stage a file by mistake. Git allows you to remove it from the staging area without deleting it.
Run:
git restore — staged hello.txtNow check the status again:
git statusYou’ll notice that the file is no longer staged.
If you want to commit it, simply stage it again:
git add .Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 8: Commit Your Changes
Now let’s save our work.
A commit is like taking a snapshot of your project. Whenever you make important changes, create a commit so you can always return to that version later.
Run:
git commit -m “Initial commit”The message inside the quotes describes what you changed.
After pressing Enter, Git will confirm that your commit was created successfully.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 9: Create a GitHub Repository
Now that your project is ready, let’s upload it to GitHub.

• Open GitHub and sign in.
2.Click the + icon in the top-right corner.
3.Select New repository.
4.Enter a repository name. (For example: GitPractice2)
5.Choose Public or Private.
6.Click Create repository.
7.GitHub will now display a page with different commands.
8.Copy the repository URL because we’ll use it in the next step.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 10: Connect Your Project to GitHub
Go back to Visual Studio Code.
In the terminal, connect your local project to GitHub using:
git remote add origin <repository-url>Replace <repository-url> with the URL you copied from GitHub.
Now upload your project by running:
git push -u origin mainAfter the push is complete, refresh your GitHub repository.
Your project is now stored both on your computer and on GitHub, making it easier to access and collaborate in the future.
You’ll now see your project files uploaded successfully.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

💡 Beginner Tip
If Git asks you to log in while pushing your project, sign in with your GitHub account and complete the authentication process. Once it’s done, your project will be uploaded successfully.
Step 11: Clone the Repository
Cloning creates a copy of a GitHub repository on your computer. This is useful when you want to work on the same project from another computer or in a different folder.

• Open your GitHub repository.

• Click the Code button.

• Make sure HTTPS is selected.

• Copy the repository URL.
Now, open Visual Studio Code and create a new folder. For this tutorial, I named it GitClonePractice.
Open this folder in VS Code and open a new terminal.
Run the following command:
git clone <repository-url>Replace <repository-url> with the URL you copied from GitHub.
Git will create another folder with your repository name and download all the project files into it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Now move into the cloned repository:
cd GitPractice2You are now inside the cloned project and can continue working with Git commands.
Step 12: Pull the Latest Changes
The git pull command downloads the latest changes from GitHub to your local project.
To understand this command, make a small change directly in your GitHub repository. For example, edit hello.txt, add a new line, and save the file.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Now return to Visual Studio Code and make sure you’re inside the cloned repository by running:
cd GitPractice2Then run:
git pull origin mainGit will download the latest changes from GitHub and update your local project.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Tip: If you see the message Already up to date, it means your local project already has the latest changes.
Step 13: Create and Merge a Branch
Branches allow you to work on new features without affecting the main project.
First, create a new branch named feature.
git branch featureNow switch to the new branch.
git checkout featurePress enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Open hello.txt and add a new line.
Save the file.
Now stage and commit the changes.
git add .git commit -m “Added a new feature”Once the commit is complete, switch back to the main branch.
git checkout mainNow merge the feature branch into the main branch.
git merge featureIf there are no conflicts, Git will display a Fast-forward message, which means the merge was completed successfully.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Finally, upload the merged changes to GitHub.
git push origin mainRefresh your GitHub repository, and you’ll see the updated project.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Conclusion
These are some of the most commonly used Git commands. Learning Git may seem challenging at first, but with regular practice, these commands become easy to remember. I hope this tutorial helps you understand the basic Git workflow and gives you the confidence to start using Git in your own projects.
Thank you for reading, and happy coding! 🚀
